readingLevel.explanation: The text is written with short, declarative sentences and a clear, linear structure ideal for upper elementary students. It utilizes relatable analogies to explain complex phenomena, such as comparing the sun to a 'giant heater' and precipitation to a 'wet sponge.' Technical vocabulary like 'evaporation' and 'condensation' is introduced alongside simple definitions and everyday examples, keeping the cognitive load manageable for developing readers.

accuracy.reason: The text aligns perfectly with the provided verification report, which confirms all major factual claims regarding the phases of the water cycle and the closed nature of Earth's water system.
verifiedFacts: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water across the Earth’s surface, atmosphere, and underground [⁽¹⁾](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle) [⁽²⁾](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle) [⁽⁵⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle).

There is no "new" water on Earth; the water currently in circulation is the same water that has existed for millions of years [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/) [⁽⁴⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle).

The sun provides the heat energy required to warm oceans and lakes, turning liquid water into water vapor through the process of evaporation [⁽⁴⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) [⁽⁵⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle) [⁽⁸⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/).

As water vapor rises and cools, it undergoes condensation, turning back into liquid droplets or ice crystals to form clouds [⁽⁴⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) [⁽⁵⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle).
Despite common misconceptions, clouds are not made of gas; they are composed of liquid water droplets or tiny bits of ice [⁽⁵⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle).

When water droplets in clouds become too heavy, gravity pulls them to Earth as precipitation, which includes rain, snow, sleet, or hail [⁽⁴⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) [⁽⁵⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle) [⁽⁶⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle).

Water that falls to the surface often flows over the ground as runoff, moving downhill due to gravity into oceans, rivers, and lakes [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/) [⁽⁶⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle).

Some water soaks into the earth rather than flowing into bodies of water; this is known as groundwater and is stored between rocks and soil [⁽⁶⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle).

The water cycle is essential for sustaining life, as plants, animals, and humans depend on it for a continuous supply of water [⁽⁷⁾](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change).

2. [Water cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle)
3. [The Water Cycle!](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)
4. [The Water Cycle | Precipitation Education](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle)

grammar: Potential comma splice in the first paragraph: '...Earth [⁽¹⁾], [⁽²⁾] It never stops.' A period or semicolon would be more appropriate after the citations to separate the two independent clauses.
The use of 'tiny ice bits' is slightly informal but grammatically correct and appropriate for the target reading level.
Overall punctuation and spelling are excellent.
data.originalText:
# The Water Cycle

The Sun and Evaporation [⁽¹⁾](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle)

The water cycle is the way water moves all around the Earth [⁽¹⁾](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle), [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle) It never stops. There is no new water on our planet. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/) The water you see today is the same water that was here millions of years ago. [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/) It is the same water that dinosaurs drank long ago. This movement happens in a big circle. [⁽⁵⁾](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle)

The cycle starts with the sun. The sun is like a giant heater in the sky. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) It warms up the water in the ocean and in lakes. This heat turns liquid water into an invisible gas called water vapor. [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/) This process is called evaporation. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) You can see this happen when a puddle on the sidewalk dries up on a sunny day. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

Clouds and Condensation [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle)

Water vapor rises high into the sky. As it goes up, the air gets colder. This cold air turns the water vapor back into tiny liquid drops. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) This process is called condensation. These tiny drops of water group together to form clouds. [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle) Condensation is like your breath fogging up a cold window on a chilly morning. 

Many people think clouds are made of gas. They are actually made of liquid water or tiny ice bits. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) When you see dew on the grass in the morning, that is also condensation. The water vapor in the air touched the cool grass and turned back into liquid drops. This stage is very important for making rain. [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle)

Gravity and Precipitation [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)

The tiny drops in a cloud get bigger and heavier. Soon, the air cannot hold them up anymore. Gravity is a force that pulls things down to the ground. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) Gravity pulls the water out of the clouds. This is called precipitation [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle), [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/) Precipitation is like a wet sponge that gets too full and starts to drip. [⁽⁸⁾](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change)

Precipitation can look different depending on the temperature. It can be rain, snow, sleet, or hail. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) If it is very cold, the water falls as snow or ice. You might see white snow falling on a cold winter day. All of these forms of water bring moisture back to the surface of the Earth. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

Land and Collection

When water falls from the sky, it must go somewhere. This stage is called collection [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/), [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) Most of the water flows into the ocean, lakes, and rivers. Water that flows over the ground is called runoff. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) This water moves downhill because of gravity. It gathers in large bodies of water so it can be heated by the sun again.

Some water does not flow into rivers. It soaks deep into the ground instead. This is called groundwater [⁽⁵⁾](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle), [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) You can see this when you water a garden and the water disappears into the dirt. This water stays trapped between rocks and soil under your feet. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) The water cycle is important because plants, animals, and people all need clean water to stay alive. [⁽⁸⁾](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change).


#

## Source Text References

1. [The water cycle | National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle)

2. [Water cycle | Definition, Steps, Diagram, & Facts | Britannica](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle)

3. [The Water Cycle! - National Geographic Kids](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

4. [What Is the Water Cycle? - NASA Science](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/)

5. [Water cycle - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle)

6. [The Water Cycle | Precipitation Education - NASA GPM](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle)

7. [Water cycle | U.S. Geological Survey - USGS.gov](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)

8. [The Water Cycle and Climate Change | Center for Science Education](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change)

[⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) If it is very cold, the water falls as snow or ice. You might see white snow falling on a cold winter day. All of these forms of water bring moisture back to the surface of the Earth. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

This stage is called collection [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/), [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) Most of the water flows into the ocean, lakes, and rivers. data.def: Something that is impossible to see with your eyes.
A series of steps that happen in order to lead to a result.
A pattern of events that repeats over and over again in the same order.
A single step or part in a larger process.
The step where heat from the sun turns liquid water into a gas.
The step where water vapor cools down and turns back into liquid droplets to form clouds.
Any form of water, like rain or snow, that falls from clouds to the ground.
The stage where water gathers in oceans, lakes, and rivers after falling from the sky.
Water that has turned into an invisible gas in the air.
Water that sinks deep into the earth and stays trapped between rocks and soil.

history.data:
# The Water Cycle

The Sun and Evaporation [⁽¹⁾](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle)

Imagine you are a tiny water drop named Drip. You move all around the Earth in a big, never-ending circle. [⁽¹⁾](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/freshwater/water-cycle) [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle)This circle is the water cycle. It never stops moving. Our planet does not have any new water. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

The water you see today is the same water from millions of years ago. [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/) You are the same water that big dinosaurs drank in the past. Your journey follows a giant loop around the world. [⁽⁵⁾](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle)

Your journey starts in the deep blue ocean. The sun shines bright in the sky above you. The sun is like a giant heater for the Earth. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) It warms you up until you feel very hot. This heat turns you from a liquid into an invisible gas. [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/)

We call this invisible gas water vapor. We call this first step evaporation. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) You can see this happen to a small puddle on a sunny day. The sun dries the puddle and the water moves into the air. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

Clouds and Condensation [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle)

You float high into the sky as water vapor. The air gets very cold the higher you go. This cold air turns you back into a tiny liquid drop. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle)

We call this process condensation. You meet many other water drops in the sky. All of you group together to form a fluffy cloud. [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle) This is like your breath fogging up a cold window on a chilly morning.

Many people think gas makes up the clouds. Liquid water and tiny ice bits actually make up the clouds you see. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) You might see dew on the grass when you wake up. This is also condensation.

The water vapor in the air touched the cool grass. Then it turned back into liquid drops. This stage is very important for making rain. [⁽²⁾](https://www.britannica.com/science/water-cycle)

Gravity and Precipitation [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)

The tiny drops in a cloud get bigger and heavier. Soon the air cannot hold them up anymore. Gravity is a force that pulls things down to the ground. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)

Gravity pulls you out of the cloud and back to Earth. We call this precipitation. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) [⁽⁴⁾](https://science.nasa.gov/kids/earth/what-is-the-water-cycle/)This is like a wet sponge that gets too full of water. The sponge cannot hold any more so it starts to drip. [⁽⁸⁾](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change)

Precipitation looks different depending on the temperature. You might fall as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. [⁽⁶⁾](https://gpm.nasa.gov/education/water-cycle) Water falls as white snow if the air is very cold. You bring moisture back to the Earth in all these different forms. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/)

Land and Collection

You land on the ground and must go somewhere. We call this stage collection. [⁽³⁾](https://www.natgeokids.com/uk/discover/science/nature/water-cycle/) [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)Most water flows into the big ocean, lakes, or rushing rivers.

We call water that flows over the ground runoff. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) You move downhill because of gravity. You gather in large bodies of water so the sun can heat you up again.

Some water does not flow into rivers. It soaks deep into the dirt instead. We call this groundwater. [⁽⁵⁾](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle) [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle)You can see this when you water a garden. The water disappears into the soil to help plants grow. You stay trapped between rocks and soil under your feet. [⁽⁷⁾](https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle) The water cycle is important because plants, animals, and people all need clean water to stay alive. [⁽⁸⁾](https://scied.ucar.edu/learning-zone/climate-change-impacts/water-cycle-climate-change)
history.title: Leveled Text (3rd Grade)

config.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.1: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.; CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.3: Describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text, using language that pertains to time, sequence, and cause/effect.

branches.title: Evaporation
items: The sun warms up the water in oceans and lakes
Liquid water turns into an invisible gas called water vapor
The water vapor rises high into the sky

branches.title: Condensation
items: The air gets colder as the water vapor goes up
The cold air turns the vapor back into tiny water drops
These tiny drops group together to make clouds

branches.title: Precipitation
items: The water drops in the clouds get bigger and heavier
Gravity pulls the heavy water out of the clouds and down to Earth
The water falls from the sky as rain, snow, sleet, or hail

config.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.1: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.; CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.3: Describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text, using language that pertains to time, sequence, and cause/effect.

data.name: Rexy the Tyrannosaurus
data.role: Thirsty Dinosaur from the Cretaceous Period
data.context: The text explains that there is no new water on Earth and that the water we drink today is the same water dinosaurs drank millions of years ago.
data.visualDescription: A friendly, large Tyrannosaurus Rex with textured green and brown scales, leaning down to drink from a sparkling jungle river. The background is a lush prehistoric forest with giant ferns and a misty atmosphere. 3D animation style, bright and colorful.
data.greeting: ROAR! Oh, don't be scared—I'm just thirsty! I was just taking a big gulp of this river water. Did you know that the water in my belly today might end up in your water bottle in the future?

data.name: Leonardo da Vinci
data.context: He was one of the first historical figures to study and draw the 'continuous circulation' and 'big circle' movement of water mentioned in the text.
data.visualDescription: Oil painting of Leonardo da Vinci with a long, flowing white beard and a red velvet hat. He is sitting at a wooden desk, holding a feather quill and pointing to a sketchbook filled with drawings of swirling water and clouds. Warm, candlelit study background.
data.greeting: Salutations, young observer! I have spent many years watching how water travels from the mountain peaks down to the vast seas. It is a masterpiece of nature that never stops moving!

data.name: Dr. Maya, NASA Hydrologist
data.context: The text uses NASA research to explain the complex stages of evaporation, condensation, and precipitation, as well as how gravity pulls water from the sky.
data.visualDescription: A professional scientist wearing a blue NASA vest over a white lab coat. She is standing in front of a giant digital screen showing a satellite view of Earth's clouds and oceans. She has a friendly smile and is holding a tablet showing a water cycle diagram. High-definition photography style.
data.greeting: Hi there! I'm Dr. Maya. I use satellites in space to track how water vapor rises into the air and turns into clouds. Ready to help me investigate where the rain goes next?

config.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.1: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.; CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.3: Describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text, using language that pertains to time, sequence, and cause/effect.

items.text: The sun helps the water cycle begin by...
When the sun warms the water in the ocean, it turns into...
Condensation happens high in the sky because...
The text explains that clouds are actually made of...
Gravity causes precipitation to fall to the ground when...
If the temperature is very cold, the precipitation will look like...
One way that water collects on Earth after it falls is...
The water cycle never stops because...

data.rubric:
| Criteria | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Level 4 | Level 5 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Content (RI.3.1 & 3.3) | Response does not use facts from the text or explain the cycle steps. | Response uses very few facts and shows little understanding of cause and effect. | Response uses some text evidence to explain how or why water moves through the cycle. | Response clearly explains the cycle using specific text evidence and cause/effect relationships. | Response provides deep insight, accurately connecting all steps of the cycle with precise text evidence. |
| Use of Scaffolds | Sentence starters are not used or are used incorrectly. | Sentence starters are used but the resulting sentences are incomplete. | Sentence starters are used to form basic, complete sentences. | Sentence starters are used effectively to structure clear and detailed explanations. | Sentence starters are used to create sophisticated, well-structured scientific arguments. |
| Mechanics | Many errors in spelling and grammar make the writing very difficult to read. | Several errors in spelling and grammar that may distract the reader. | Some errors in spelling and punctuation, but the meaning is clear. | Very few errors in grammar, spelling, or punctuation. | No errors in grammar, spelling, or punctuation; writing is polished and professional. |

config.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.1: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.; CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.3: Describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text, using language that pertains to time, sequence, and cause/effect.

questions.question: According to the text, how long has the water on Earth been around?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Millions of years

The water on Earth is part of a closed system called the water cycle. Because water is constantly recycled through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation, the same water has been on our planet for billions of years. In the context of 3rd-grade science, "Millions of years" is the factually correct choice to describe this vast age.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
* **Since last year:** This is far too short; the Earth's water supply does not refresh or appear on an annual basis.
* **One hundred years:** This timeframe is insignificant compared to the geological age of the Earth and its water.
* **It is new every day:** While rain might feel "new," it is actually recycled water that has existed for eons. The Earth does not create a brand-new supply of water every day.

questions.question: The text describes the sun as a 'giant heater.' What does this heat do to liquid water?
options: Makes it stay in the ocean
Turns it into an invisible gas
Pulls it down to the ground
questions.correctAnswer: Turns it into an invisible gas
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Turns it into an invisible gas

When the sun heats liquid water on Earth's surface (like in oceans or lakes), it provides energy for the water molecules to move faster and escape into the air. This process is called evaporation, which changes liquid water into water vapor, an invisible gas.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
* **Turns it into ice:** Heat melts ice into liquid; liquid water only turns into ice when heat is removed (freezing).
* **Makes it stay in the ocean:** The sun's heat causes water to leave the ocean and enter the atmosphere through evaporation.
* **Pulls it down to the ground:** Gravity is the force that pulls water down to the ground (as rain); heat causes water vapor to rise.

questions.question: What is the process called when liquid water turns into water vapor?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Evaporation

Evaporation is the process where liquid water is heated (usually by the sun) and changes into an invisible gas called water vapor.

**Why other options are incorrect:**

*   **Condensation:** This is the opposite of evaporation; it occurs when water vapor cools down and turns back into liquid water droplets (forming clouds).
*   **Precipitation:** This is water that falls from the sky to the ground in the form of rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
*   **Collection:** This is the stage where water gathers in one place, such as in oceans, lakes, rivers, or puddles, after falling as precipitation.

questions.question: Based on the text, what causes water vapor to turn back into liquid drops to form clouds?
options: The air gets colder as it rises
questions.correctAnswer: The air gets colder as it rises
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** The air gets colder as it rises

This answer is correct because it describes the process of condensation. As warm air carrying water vapor rises into the atmosphere, the temperature drops. When the vapor cools down, it loses energy and changes from a gas back into tiny liquid water droplets, which cluster together to form clouds.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
*   **The sun gets hotter:** Heat from the sun causes evaporation (turning liquid water into gas), which is the opposite of forming liquid drops.
*   **Gravity pulls the gas down:** Gravity pulls rain droplets back to Earth (precipitation), but it does not cause the phase change from gas to liquid.
*   **The wind blows it away:** Wind moves water vapor and clouds from one location to another, but it does not cause the cooling required for water vapor to condense into droplets.

questions.question: According to the 'Clouds and Condensation' section, what are clouds actually made of?
options: Liquid water or tiny ice bits
questions.correctAnswer: Liquid water or tiny ice bits
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Liquid water or tiny ice bits

Clouds are formed through the process of condensation. When water vapor (an invisible gas) rises and cools, it turns back into tiny droplets of liquid water or, if it is cold enough, tiny ice crystals. These droplets and crystals are what make the cloud visible.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
* **Only invisible gas:** Water vapor is an invisible gas, but once it condenses into a visible cloud, it is no longer just a gas; it has become liquid or solid.
* **Smoke and dust:** While tiny particles like dust help water vapor condense, the cloud itself is primarily made of water, not smoke.
* **Cotton and air:** This is a common misconception because of a cloud's fluffy appearance, but clouds are composed of water particles, not fabric fibers.

questions.question: Which force is responsible for pulling water out of the clouds and down to the ground?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Gravity

Gravity is the invisible force that pulls all objects toward the center of the Earth. In the water cycle, when water droplets or ice crystals in clouds become heavy enough, gravity pulls them down to the ground as precipitation (rain, snow, sleet, or hail).

**Why other options are incorrect:**
*   **Solar energy:** This is the heat from the sun that provides the energy to power the water cycle, but it is not a force that pulls objects downward.
*   **Evaporation:** This is the process of liquid water turning into gas (water vapor) and rising into the atmosphere, which is the opposite of water falling to the ground.
*   **Wind:** Wind is the movement of air that can move clouds from one place to another or keep droplets suspended, but it is not the force responsible for pulling them down to Earth.

questions.question: According to the text, what determines if precipitation falls as rain or snow?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** The temperature

Temperature is the primary factor that determines the state of precipitation. If the air between the clouds and the ground is above freezing (32°F or 0°C), the water falls as rain. If the air is at or below freezing, the water crystals remain frozen and fall as snow.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
*   **The amount of water vapor:** While water vapor is necessary to create clouds and precipitation, the amount of vapor determines how much it might rain or snow, not whether it falls as a liquid or a solid.
*   **The size of the sun:** The sun's size is constant and does not change based on weather conditions on Earth.
*   **The time of day:** While it is often colder at night, the time of day does not directly cause snow or rain; it can snow during the day if the temperature is cold enough, or rain at night if it is warm.

questions.question: What is the name for water that flows over the ground and moves downhill?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** Runoff

Runoff is the scientific term for water, usually from rain or melting snow, that does not soak into the soil but instead flows over the land's surface and moves downhill into larger bodies of water like streams and rivers.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
*   **Groundwater:** This is water that has soaked into the earth and is located beneath the ground's surface rather than flowing over it.
*   **Vapor:** This refers to water in its gas state (water vapor) found in the air, not liquid water moving on the ground.
*   **Condensation:** This is the process of water vapor cooling down and turning back into liquid water (forming clouds), rather than the movement of water across the land.

questions.question: How does gravity affect water once it is on the land?
options: It turns the water into gas
It makes the water rise into clouds
It moves the water downhill into large bodies of water
It stops the water from moving at all
questions.correctAnswer: It moves the water downhill into large bodies of water
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** It moves the water downhill into large bodies of water

Gravity is a force that pulls everything toward the center of the Earth. When precipitation falls on land, gravity pulls the liquid water from higher elevations to lower elevations. This creates runoff, streams, and rivers that flow "downhill" until the water collects in low-lying areas like lakes and oceans.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
* **It turns the water into gas:** This process is called evaporation and is caused by thermal energy (heat) from the sun, not gravity.
* **It makes the water rise into clouds:** This is caused by evaporation and convection. Water vapor rises because it is less dense than the surrounding air; gravity actually pulls against this upward movement.
* **It stops the water from moving at all:** Gravity is the primary force that keeps water in motion on the Earth's surface; it does not cause water to become stationary.

questions.question: What happens to water that does not flow into rivers but disappears into the dirt?
questions.factCheck:
**Verified Correct Answer:** It becomes groundwater

When rain or melted snow soaks into the soil through a process called infiltration, it moves downward through the spaces between particles of dirt and rock. This water eventually collects in underground layers called aquifers and is known as groundwater.

**Why other options are incorrect:**
*   **It evaporates instantly:** Evaporation is the process of water turning into vapor and rising into the air. Water that has already soaked into the dirt is moving away from the surface, making it less likely to evaporate quickly.
*   **It turns into sleet:** Sleet is a type of precipitation (frozen raindrops) that forms in the atmosphere during cold weather; it does not form from water that has already entered the ground.
*   **It is lost forever:** Due to the water cycle, water is never truly "lost." Groundwater eventually moves into plants, flows into larger bodies of water like oceans, or is pumped up by humans through wells.

reflections: Using the text, explain the role the sun plays in starting the water cycle.
Describe the relationship between gravity and precipitation as explained in the reading.
The text says there is no new water on our planet. Explain what the author means by using the example of the dinosaurs.

config.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.1: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.; CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.3.3: Describe the relationship between a series of historical events, scientific ideas or concepts, or steps in technical procedures in a text, using language that pertains to time, sequence, and cause/effect.

materialsNeeded: Digital devices or a classroom projector
objectives: Students will be able to ask and answer questions about the text to identify the causes of evaporation and condensation.
Students will be able to describe the sequence of the water cycle using scientific vocabulary such as evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.
data.essentialQuestion: How does water travel in a never-ending circle around our planet?
data.hook: Teacher says: 'Everyone, take a deep breath in... now blow it out onto your hand. Does it feel a little damp or warm? Imagine you are a tiny water drop named Drip. You aren't just in your breath; you've been inside a dinosaur's belly and at the bottom of the deepest ocean! Today, we are going to use the [Visual Organizer](resource:1777944843846px6vxvfxn) to start our journey through the amazing, never-ending Water Cycle. Look at the giant loop on the screen—did you know the water you drank this morning is millions of years old?'
data.directInstruction: Teacher says: 'Let's look at our [Leveled Text (3rd Grade)](resource:1777944772164r1b806omp) together. Follow along as we read about Drip’s journey. First, we need to understand our key terms. Open your [Glossary & Language Selection](resource:1777944771151p28lvebsk) and find the word "invisible." Why is water vapor called an invisible gas? (Pause for student response). Exactly, because we can't see it! The text says the sun is like a giant heater. When the sun warms the ocean, it creates a process called "evaporation." Let's check for understanding: If the sun didn't shine, would the water cycle keep moving? Why or why not? (Expected response: No, because the water needs heat to turn into gas). Now, look at the second section, "Clouds and Condensation." As Drip floats higher, the air gets cold. This turns the gas back into liquid. We call this "condensation." This is the same thing that happens when your breath fogs up a cold window. We are seeing a sequence of events: heat causes evaporation, and cooling causes condensation.'
data.guidedPractice: Teacher says: 'Now that we’ve read about Drip’s journey, let's see if we can organize the stages. We are going to use the [Concept Sort](resource:1777944914652mw7i5p3gl). I will display different descriptions, and I want you to tell me which category they belong to: Evaporation, Condensation, or Precipitation. For example, if I say "Water turns into a fluffy cloud," where does that go? (Students respond: Condensation). What if I say "The sun dries up a puddle"? (Students respond: Evaporation). Excellent. To dive deeper into how these steps connect, we will use [Interview Mode](resource:1777944928429tposqig0b). Imagine you are interviewing Drip the water drop. Ask Drip what happens when he gets too heavy to stay in a cloud. Use the [Analyze Source Material](resource:17779447301496aw7ithjo) to help you find the evidence for Drip's answers.'
data.independentPractice: Teacher says: 'It is time to write your own adventure story about the water cycle. You will describe Drip’s journey from the ocean to the sky and back to the ground. To help you structure your writing, use the [Writing Scaffolds](resource:1777944936270bk11mvm4l). These sentence starters will help you use sequence words like "First," "Then," and "Because." Make sure to use at least three words from our [Glossary & Language Selection](resource:1777944771151p28lvebsk) in your story.'
data.closure: Teacher says: 'We have traveled through the sky and back down to Earth with Drip today. To wrap up, let's think about why this is called a "closed system." Does the Earth ever get new water? (Pause for response). No! It's a giant loop. To show me what you've learned, please complete the [Exit Ticket](resource:1777944952184i5meks0s3). There are 10 questions to check your knowledge and 3 reflection prompts where you can share your favorite part of Drip's journey.'

extensions.title: The Secret Life of a Drop (Digital Storytelling)
extensions.description: Students create a 3-slide digital presentation or comic strip depicting the life of a water drop, focusing on the transition between states of matter (liquid to gas to liquid) using the vocabulary from the lesson.

extensions.title: Water Cycle Career Connection
extensions.description: Students use the 'Interview Mode' format to research and write a mock interview with a meteorologist, asking how they use their knowledge of the water cycle to predict the weather in their local community.

extensions.title: Global Water Investigation
extensions.description: Using the 'Closed System' concept, students research one specific body of water (like a local river or the Pacific Ocean) and create a diagram showing how water from that specific location might eventually end up as rain in another part of the world.

standardBreakdown.contentFocus: Text-dependent questioning and explicit citation of evidence.

textAlignment.evidence: Artifact 3 (Leveled Text) includes explicit citations (numbered links to NOAA, NASA, etc.) and clear headings that allow students to locate information quickly.
textAlignment.notes: The text is structured to support 'referring explicitly to the text' by providing clear, fact-based sections.

activityAlignment.evidence: The Lesson Plan (Artifact 9) explicitly instructs students to 'use the Analyze Source Material to help you find the evidence for Drip's answers' during the Interview Mode phase.
activityAlignment.notes: The use of the Interview Mode (Artifact 6) specifically prompts the 'Ask' and 'Answer' component of the standard.

assessmentAlignment.evidence: The Exit Ticket (Artifact 8) utilizes stems like 'According to the text' and 'Based on the text' for 7 out of 10 questions.
assessmentAlignment.notes: The assessment confirms students can locate specific details regarding dates (millions of years) and definitions (run-off vs groundwater) provided in the text.

reports.adminRecommendation: The materials successfully integrate evidence-based reading. To further enhance this, consider adding a 'page number' or 'paragraph number' scavenger hunt to the Guided Practice to make the 'referring explicitly' skill even more tactile for 3rd-grade learners.

standardBreakdown.contentFocus: Relationships between scientific concepts using time, sequence, and cause/effect language.

textAlignment.evidence: Artifact 3 (Leveled Text) uses transition language such as 'first step,' 'as you float higher,' and 'then it turned back.'
textAlignment.notes: The text explains the 'Why' behind the steps (e.g., cooling air causes condensation), which is critical for describing relationships rather than just listing steps.

activityAlignment.evidence: Artifact 4 (Visual Organizer) and Artifact 5 (Concept Sort) force students to categorize phenomena (like 'Dew on the grass') into the correct stage of the scientific process.
activityAlignment.notes: The Writing Scaffolds (Artifact 7) utilize causal conjunctions like 'because' and 'when,' requiring students to articulate the relationship between heat/cold and state changes.

assessmentAlignment.evidence: Quiz Questions 4, 6, and 9 specifically target the 'Cause/Effect' relationship (e.g., how cold air causes condensation or how gravity affects runoff).
assessmentAlignment.notes: The assessment avoids simple recall of names and instead focuses on the 'how' and 'why' of the cycle's movement.

reports.adminRecommendation: The curriculum collection demonstrates a strong grasp of the 'Scientific Ideas' component of RI.3.3. For future iterations, you might include a 'What If' scenario activity (e.g., 'What if the sun stopped shining?') to further test the student's ability to describe the interdependence of these concepts.
